topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something unwelcome. Professor Albright was departing. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent light of my cramped dorm room buzzing overhead, suddenly feeling a profound sense of emptiness. I traced the lines of his name with my finger, a faint, bitter taste rising in my throat. He had been the architect of my entire research trajectory, the one person who had somehow managed to see the potential in my meandering ideas. Now, that structure was crumbling. I pushed my chair back, the scrape against the linoleum echoing the sudden, sharp deflation in my chest. I picked up my notebook, the pages feeling suddenly heavy and pointless. I didn't bother to reply to the automated farewell message; there was nothing left to say, only a cold, hard certainty that whatever came next would be significantly less interesting.

[story 2]

I watched the campus news feed with a practiced air of boredom, my expression carefully neutral as the announcement about Dr. Albright’s resignation scrolled across the screen. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening began around my jaw. It wasn't anger, not exactly; it was more like the unpleasant sensation of finding something spoiled. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric protesting under my weight, and let a slow, humorless smile touch my lips. *Of course,* I thought, the word tasting like ash. He had always operated on a level entirely separate from the rest of us, a rarefied air I could never quite breathe. I picked up a pen, tapping it rhythmically against my desk—a steady, irritating beat against the silence of my mounting frustration. This was just another administrative shift, another piece of the university machinery grinding along, completely oblivious to the seismic shift it caused for those of us actually trying to do meaningful work.

[story 3]

When I finally read the memo, I didn't move for a long time. My hands, resting on the keyboard, clenched into tight fists, the knuckles turning white. The air in the library seemed suddenly too thick, suffocating. I reread the lines, focusing intently on the formal language, searching for any hint of sincerity that wasn't just bureaucratic boilerplate. There was none. It was just a procedural exit, a convenient rearrangement of personnel. A wave of profound indifference washed over me, so complete it felt like numbness. I stood up abruptly, the movement stiff, and walked toward the window. Outside, the autumn leaves were falling, a chaotic, beautiful display that mocked the rigid, predictable nature of the academic world. I didn't feel sadness; I felt a quiet, absolute dismissal of everything he represented.
